subject: Physics
solution:
Given:
object using the lens formula:
\(\begin{aligned}& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v \prime}-\frac{1}{w^{\prime}} \\\Rightarrow & \frac{21}{240} \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{3}{35} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}} \\\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u^{\prime}} & =\frac{3}{35} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{21}{240} \mathrm{~cm} \\\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{5}\left[\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{21}{48} \mathrm{~cm}\right] \\\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u^{\prime}} & =\frac{1}{5}\left[\frac{144-147}{336}\right] \\\Rightarrow u^{\prime} & =-560 \mathrm{~cm}=-5.6 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}\)
Hence, the object should be at \(5.6 \mathrm{~m}\) in front of the lens.
Hence, the oblect should be moved by 5.6-24= \(3.2 \mathrm{~cm}\).

subject: Physics
solution:
Given,

The diameter of the objective lens, D=24.4 cm

The wavelength of the light, λ=2440 A∘

The resolving power of a telescope is given by,

R.P=D1.22λ ⇒R.P=24.4×10-21.22×2440×10-10=8.2×105

Hence, the resolving power of a telescope is 8.2×105.

Location of image of $\mathrm{A}:-$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{60} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=60 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~m}=2\end{aligned}$

$\therefore$ Angle made with principle axis $=-45^{\circ}$

subject: Physics
solution:
For $\delta_{\min }$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{e} \\ & \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{r}_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \\ & \frac{\delta_{\min }}{\mathrm{A}}=1 \\ & \frac{2 \mathrm{i}-\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1 \\ & \\ & 2 \mathrm{i}=2 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{A} \end{aligned}$
Snell's law $\begin{aligned} & 1 \times \sin i=\mu \sin r \\ & \sin i=\mu \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & \sin A=\mu \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & 2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cos \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{3} \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{A}{2}=30^{\circ} \\ & \therefore A=60^{\circ} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let C be the critical angle.

Then, sinC=1μ, where, μ is the refractive index of glass.

So, sinC=12⇒C=45°

As the angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle, so using Snell's law,

 sinC=μsinθ

⇒12=2 sinθ

⇒θ=30°

Now, lateral displacement of the ray is 

x=t sin(i-r) sec r

⇒x=3sin 45°-30°sec 30°

⇒x=3(0.26)23

⇒x=0.52 cm

⇒x=52×10-2 cm

⇒sin90°-A2=sinδm+A2

Therefore, 90°-A2=δm+A2  ⇒δm=180°-2A

subject: Physics
solution:
As per sign conventions, object distance uA=-45 cm, focal length f=-20 cm.

From the mirror formula

1vA+1uA=1f⇒1vA+1-45=1-20⇒vA=-45×(-20)-45-(-20)=-90025⇒vA=-36 cm

And uB=-35 cm

1vB+1uB=1f⇒1vB+1-35=1-20⇒vB=-35×(-20)-35-(-20)=700-15⇒vB=-1403 cm

Now, length of image=vA-vB

=-36+1403 cm

=-108+1403 cm

=323 cm

∴ x=32

subject: Physics
solution:
Given an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 60^{\circ}$.
The refractive index of the prism is $\mu = \sqrt{2}$.
The emergent ray grazes the second surface, which means the angle of emergence is $e = 90^{\circ}$.
Applying Snell's law at the second surface: $\mu \sin(r_2) = 1 \cdot \sin(e)$.
$\sqrt{2} \sin(r_2) = \sin(90^{\circ}) = 1$.
$\sin(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, which gives $r_2 = 45^{\circ}$.
For a prism, we know the relation $r_1 + r_2 = A$.
Substituting the values: $r_1 + 45^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}$.
$r_1 = 60^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} = 15^{\circ}$.
The angle of refraction at the incident surface is $r_1 = 15^{\circ}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Relative refractive index can be found as,

μ2μ1=v1v2=1.52=34.

When the angle of incident is greater than the critical angle total internal reflection occurs. If the critical angle is ic then,

sinic=μ2μ1=34

Now tanic=342-32=37

Therefore, ic=tan-137

subject: Physics
solution:
Equivalent focal length
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2} \\ & =\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{2-3}{60}=-\frac{1}{60} \\ & f=-60 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
Lens formula
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{-60} \\ & \mathrm{v}=-15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$

From question, $\frac{\ell^{\prime 2}}{\ell}=9$

$\mathrm{u}=-40 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{v}=3 \times 40=120 \mathrm{~cm}$
$$
\mathrm{f}=?
$$
From formula, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{120}-\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\
& \text { or, } \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{120}+\frac{1}{40}=\frac{1+3}{120} \therefore \mathrm{f}=30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$
